concept: What the machine sees: an imaging-sonar COUNTING SCREEN on the Kenai. We watch the river through the machine's own display as salmon rise up an acoustic beam, get tallied at a count line, then boxed and classified by computer vision in real time. The honest caveat is shown, not said: the machine still misses, and the decision of how many fish to let pass before the nets open stays human.
honest_caveat: Counting is not managing. The AI counts the run; the escapement call (open or hold the nets) is still a human decision.
references: a real ARIS/DIDSON sonar operator display
Bloomberg/Vox editorial-data clarity for the run-curve + labels

fingerprint.palette: amber acoustic echoes on abyssal black water-column, cool cyan UI chrome, a single warning-coral accent for the error
fingerprint.metaphor: the machine counting the run — each fish becomes a boxed, classified echo the instant it trips the count line

palette: amber acoustic echoes on abyssal black water-column, cool cyan UI chrome, a single warning-coral accent for the error
divergence_note: Deliberately opposite to the last two on every structural axis. The beluga was an eye-level NATURALISTIC underwater scene with a SINGLE organic hero swimming HORIZONTALLY across a centered, lower-third layout. The permafrost was a side-on CROSS-SECTION cutaway of the ground descending VERTICALLY in a full-bleed SPLIT. This one is the MACHINE'S POV — we look AT a sonar SCREEN (instrument-readout register), at a MULTIPLICITY of fish (a whole run, not one hero) RISING up a beam through a HUD instrument frame. Different camera, different subject-count, different motion direction, different layout, different register, different color world (amber-on-black + cyan, never the teal/aurora or thermal duotone), and a different central metaphor (counting/classification, not a creature portrait or a digital twin). If you muted all three back to back, no one would call them the same template.

beats.new: Cold open on a dark sonar display; range arcs ghost in, the beam fan powers up from a transducer at bottom-center, one lone amber echo drifts up and trips a faint COUNT LINE -> tally ticks 1.
beats.means: States the premise visually in one second: this is a machine that counts fish.

beats.new: A turbid gray haze wipes across and a faint sun-disc at the top dims out; the display switches to SONAR mode and echoes resolve OUT of the noise as the beam brightens.
beats.transition: focus-pull from optical murk to acoustic clarity
beats.means: Why sound, not light: the river is too gray to see into.

beats.new: Instrument furniture discloses: range arcs label in meters, 'ARIS . KENAI RIVER . since 2010' stamps in, a steady stream of salmon echoes rises up the beam leaving brief comet trails, time-scrub readout starts advancing.
beats.means: Establishes the real instrument and that every passing fish is a bright echo.

beats.new: A human CROSSHAIR/reticle appears, moves deliberately to ONE fish, pauses, clicks a tally (+1), moves to the next - slow, one at a time. 'COUNTED BY HAND' label.
beats.transition: reticle eases in, snaps to target
beats.means: For years a person did this by hand, frame by frame.

beats.new: More fish enter than the reticle can tag; uncounted echoes pile toward the top edge while the crosshair stutters between targets.
beats.means: The hand-count cannot keep up - the bottleneck is visible.

beats.new: HARD CUT on the downbeat: the reticle vanishes and detection BOXES snap onto EVERY fish at once (corner brackets + confidence), in parallel; the count line increments rapidly.
beats.transition: hard cut on the stressed word; one-at-a-time -> all-at-once
beats.means: Computer vision takes the job: a box around each fish as it swims upstream. The hero moment.

beats.new: Boxes gain SPECIES labels - 'KING' on the long echoes, 'SOCKEYE' on the smaller - and a length caliper flicks across one fish to show the basis; a small legend appears.
beats.means: It tells a king from a sockeye by length - the same trick biologists use.

beats.new: The single screen pulls back and multiplies: a strip of small river-screens lights up across a faint BC-to-AK map line, 'RIVERS . BC TO ALASKA . 500K+ LABELED FRAMES'.
beats.transition: pull-back / mask into a multi-screen strip
beats.means: The approach is spreading: computer vision counts salmon on rivers from British Columbia to Alaska, trained on hundreds of thousands of labeled frames (532k in the BC system, Atlas et al. 2023).

beats.new: The strip collapses back to the one hero screen; a 'TRAINED' progress bar completes.
beats.means: Bridge: settle from the scale-out back to the working instrument.

beats.new: THE MISS: a fish passes through the beam with NO box - it slips by unclassified; the tally stutters, a coral '~ -25%' flag blinks, and one box flickers its confidence 0.94 -> 0.71.
beats.transition: a beat of stutter; coral warning blink
beats.means: The honest caveat, shown not said: the machine still misses (a 2025 Kenai test came up about a quarter short).

beats.new: A season RUN-CURVE that has been climbing in the corner comes forward, rising toward a dashed ESCAPEMENT GOAL line; the live count feeds it.
beats.transition: graph eases forward to hero scale
beats.means: Counting feeds a bigger question - how full is the run against the goal.

beats.new: The curve reaches the goal line; 'HOW MANY BEFORE THE NETS OPEN?' crystallizes; the AI boxes DIM and a human-owned OPEN / HOLD toggle stays lit on the goal line.
beats.transition: AI layer dims; human toggle holds
beats.means: The thesis: counting was never the hard part - that call is still ours.

beats.new: Outro begins: the sonar UI recedes and the ALASKA.AI wordmark resolves center.
Tagline reveals: 'what's moving in alaska ai, this week'.
The last amber echo drifts up and out of frame; gentle cinematic fade to black.
beats.transition: fade out, motion to the final frame
beats.means: A real ending; never a static hold.

ending: Branded outro (wordmark -> tagline) staged after the VO, with the final echo still rising and a slow fade. Motion runs to the last frame (EVENT_CADENCE).
captions: voice-synced kinetic phrases from audio/words60.json (CAPTION_SYNC gate)
voice: edge_tts:en-US-BrianMultilingualNeural (draft); publish in a Kokoro voice
music: freshly sourced per-run, credited (DISPATCH_MUSIC); MUSIC gate rejects synth
